title: Fitting a solid lipped edging strip to a shelf
subtitle: Fix a hardwood or softwood lipping to a raw MDF or plywood shelf edge
excerpt: How to glue and pin a solid timber lipping to the front edge of an MDF or plywood shelf to cover the raw edge and give a finished appearance. Covers cutting the lipping, applying PVA, clamping, flushing, and finishing.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1905, public domain). Cabinet-making practice, City and Guilds Level 2 Furniture-making. Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989).

glossaryTerms.definition: A strip of solid timber glued and sometimes pinned to the raw edge of a sheet-material shelf or panel. Its purpose is to protect the vulnerable edge, to accept paint or stain better than raw MDF (which absorbs too much finish to achieve a clean painted edge), and to allow the edge to be planed and sanded to a finished surface. Standard lipping thicknesses are 6 mm, 9 mm, and 12 mm; width matches the panel thickness.

content.text: The raw front edge of an MDF or plywood shelf is difficult to finish: the edge grain of MDF absorbs far more paint than the face, producing a rough, dull edge that never matches the face finish. A solid timber

glued to the edge gives a surface that planes smooth, takes paint cleanly, and protects the panel edge from damage. The lipping is proud of both faces when first glued on and is flushed to the panel after the glue sets.

Solid timber lipping (softwood for painted shelves, hardwood for oiled or stained shelves) cut to the shelf thickness. PVA woodworking glue. Panel pins (25 mm). G-cramps or sash cramps (or masking tape as a budget clamp). A smoothing plane or block plane. 120-grit and 180-grit sandpaper. A combination square for checking flush. A punch for setting pins below the surface.
Step 1, cut and prepare the lipping

Cut the lipping to the exact shelf length; a clean straight cut at 90 degrees. The lipping width should be 1 to 2 mm wider than the shelf thickness so it is slightly proud on both faces when glued. Apply PVA wood glue to both mating surfaces: the lipping back and the shelf front edge. Allow the glue to tack for 60 seconds (open time), then press the lipping firmly onto the edge. Clamp with G-cramps at 150 mm intervals, protecting the lipping face with scrap timber pads under the clamp jaws.

On long shelves (over 600 mm), drive 25 mm panel pins through the lipping face into the shelf edge at 200 mm intervals to prevent the lipping sliding while the clamps are in place. Use the pin hammer and keep the pins to the centre of the lipping width. Punch the pin heads below the surface and fill the holes with wood filler after the glue dries. Allow the glue to dry fully; at least 2 hours for PVA, or as stated on the tin.
Step 3, flush the lipping to the shelf faces

Once cured, remove the cramps. The lipping will be slightly proud of both shelf faces. Use the smoothing plane to bring the lipping flush with the top shelf face, taking fine shavings with the grain. Check with the combination square and a steel rule; the lipping should be perfectly flush with the face, not below it. Repeat on the underside. Finish by sanding with 120-grit, then 180-grit, running the sanding block along the grain. The transition between lipping and shelf face should be imperceptible.

attrs.symptom: Lipping slides out of position while clamping
attrs.cause: PVA acts as a lubricant before it sets; the lipping slides under clamp pressure.
attrs.fix: Drive two 25 mm panel pins before clamping to locate the lipping. Alternatively, rub chalk on the shelf edge before applying glue — the chalk grip prevents sliding.

attrs.cause: Planing against the grain of the lipping timber.
attrs.fix: Check the grain direction before planing. If the plane is tearing the grain, reverse the plane direction or reduce the depth of cut to the thinnest possible shaving.
